name: Production Agent Engineering
scope: A person with ADVANCED proficiency level in Production Agent Engineering should be able to design, build, and operate robust agent systems across the full lifecycle, including architecture (frontend channels, API layers, orchestrators, tool services, data stores), advanced agent patterns (ReAct, planner-executor, reflection, multi-agent), and integration with enterprise systems (databases, APIs, cloud, CRM/ERP). They deeply understand LLM fundamentals (tokenization, context windows, temperature, model classes, prompt engineering, tool/function calling), retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and memory/state management (short/long-term, episodic/semantic, vector DBs, indexing, knowledge freshness). They engineer multi-step planning, task decomposition, reasoning (prompt-based, hybrid, external planners), and robust tool integrations (schema validation, error handling, transactional integrity, rate limits, auth). They implement safety, alignment, and guardrails (system prompts, filters, moderation, attack defense, escalation, compliance), reliability and observability (logging, tracing, metrics, SLOs, disaster recovery), and evaluation/testing (unit, scenario, regression, LLM-as-judge, human eval, golden sets). They optimize for performance, scaling, and cost (model selection, caching, parallelism, load testing, multi-model routing), manage data/feedback loops (logging, feedback, improvement, safe A/B testing), and ensure security, privacy, and compliance (input validation, secrets, access control, PII, audit). They apply MLOps/release management (CI/CD, model/version management, feature flags, reproducibility), leverage major agent and LLM orchestration frameworks and cloud/container infra, and collaborate effectively with product, engineering, legal, and risk teams. They produce clear documentation, mentor others, contribute to knowledge bases, and participate in governance, standards, and risk management processes for agent systems.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core Agent & LLM Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Understands end‑to‑end lifecycle of production agents (from problem framing to design, deployment, monitoring, and iteration)
- Precisely distinguishes and leverages different agent types:
  - Stateless vs stateful agents
  - Single‑shot vs multi‑step workflow agents
  - Tool‑using agents vs pure conversational agents
  - Reactive vs deliberative/planning agents
  - Single‑agent systems vs multi‑agent systems
- Deep command of LLM fundamentals as they apply to agents:
  - Tokenization, context windows, truncation and information loss
  - Model capabilities vs limitations (hallucination, brittleness, non‑determinism)
  - Temperature, top‑p, top‑k, logit bias, response length controls
  - Latency, throughput characteristics of different model classes (chat, completion, function‑calling, reasoning models)
  - Foundation vs instruction‑tuned vs domain‑tuned models
- Uses prompt engineering not as a bag of tricks but as a systematic design discipline:
  - Roles, instructions, constraints, examples, and formatting contracts
  - Multi‑prompt pipelines and prompt templates for different steps and tools
  - Robust handling of user intent, ambiguity, and constraints in prompts
  - Explicit contracts for tool usage and output schemas
- Clear mental models for when to use agents vs conventional software:
  - Cost/benefit and risk analysis for automation with agents
  - Identifying tasks that are brittle, high‑risk, or not suitable for LLMs
  - Hybrid designs where agents are used only for narrow sub‑tasks
- Strong understanding of retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG) as a backbone for many agents:
  - When RAG is required vs when base model knowledge suffices
  - Trade‑offs between retrieval, planning, and external tool calls
  - RAG failure modes and their impact on agent reliability
- Solid grounding in metrics for agent behavior:
  - Task success, completion rate, correctness, safety violations
  - User satisfaction, latency, cost per task, escalation rate","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Production Agent Architectures & Patterns","scope_included":"- Can design and critique full architectures for production agent systems, including:
  - Frontend channels (web, mobile, chat, email, voice, system‑to‑system)
  - API layers, orchestrators, workers, tool services, data stores
  - Asynchronous and event‑driven flows where required
- Applies and adapts common agent patterns with clarity:
  - ReAct / Tool‑augmented reasoning patterns
  - Planner–executor (planning + tool execution + refinement)
  - Reflection / self‑critique and repair loops
  - Decomposition and sub‑task routing agents
  - Guardrail agents, policy agents, and safety filters
  - Multi‑agent collaboration vs single orchestrator with tools
- Designs for production constraints:
  - Predictable latencies and timeouts across multi‑step plans
  - Circuit breakers and fallbacks for tool failures, model failures, or upstream dependencies
  - Idempotency and safe retries for tool calls and workflows
  - Back‑pressure handling and queueing designs
- Chooses appropriate orchestration layer:
  - Framework‑level orchestration vs bespoke state machines
  - Synchronous HTTP flows vs background job queues and schedulers
  - Event streaming for long‑running or high‑volume agents
- Understands trade‑offs among architecture options:
  - Centralized vs decentralized tool routing
  - Monolithic vs microservice‑style tool services
  - Direct tool calls from client vs mediated by agent backend
- Designs agents that integrate both deterministic logic and nondeterministic model behavior:
  - Clear separation of concerns between rule‑based parts and LLM‑driven parts
  - Well‑defined API contracts around fuzzy LLM steps
  - Deterministic post‑processing and validation around non‑deterministic responses","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Task Decomposition, Planning & Reasoning","scope_included":"- Can engineer agents that perform non‑trivial multi‑step tasks reliably:
  - Complex workflows (e.g., multi‑system updates, investigations, analysis tasks)
  - Long‑running tasks with intermittent human/agent interaction
- Designs and tunes planning mechanisms:
  - Prompt‑based planning within the LLM
  - External planners (classical workflows, rule engines, BPM systems) orchestrating LLM steps
  - Hybrid approaches (LLM proposes, deterministic system validates and enforces constraints)
- Structures task decomposition with explicit representations:
  - Plans as JSON or DSLs
  - Explicit step definitions, dependencies, and success criteria
  - Intermediate state and artifacts management
- Ensures reasoning robustness:
  - Encourages chain‑of‑thought or structured reasoning where appropriate (while handling cost and latency)
  - Uses reflection and critique steps to catch obvious reasoning errors
  - Designs targeted prompts for edge cases and hard constraints
- Handles context and information gathering:
  - Clarification questions when requirements are ambiguous or incomplete
  - Iterative refinement: propose → get feedback → refine
  - Distinguishing between known unknowns (ask / retrieve) and unknown unknowns (escalate)
- Designs arbitration and decision rules:
  - Confidence estimation mechanisms (implicit via LLM or explicit scoring)
  - When to stop, when to escalate to humans, when to fall back to deterministic flows
  - Policies for conflicting or incomplete evidence","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Tooling, Integrations & Environment Interfaces (Core Agent Skills)","scope_included":"- Deeply understands "tools" as first‑class entities in agent design:
  - Tool schemas, input/output contracts, descriptions, and capabilities
  - Categorization of tools (data retrieval, write operations, analytic, transactional, external APIs)
  - Tool discovery, selection, and routing strategies
- Designs robust tool integrations:
  - Validation and sanitization of LLM‑generated tool arguments
  - Strong typing and schema validation for tool inputs and outputs
  - Error handling, retries, backoff, and idempotency considerations for each tool
  - Timeout and cancellation management for slow or flaky tools
- Integrates agents with typical enterprise systems and services:
  - Databases and data warehouses
  - CRM, ERP, ticketing, and HR systems
  - Cloud services (storage, queues, serverless, monitoring)
  - Internal REST/GraphQL/gRPC APIs and service meshes
- Manages side effects and transactional integrity:
  - Designing compensation actions or rollbacks
  - Handling partial success and multi‑step updates safely
  - Ensuring that LLM‑driven decisions do not violate critical business constraints
- Implements and configures tool‑calling capabilities of modern LLMs:
  - Function/tool calling schemas and system prompts
  - Multi‑tool calls within a single step vs iterative reasoning with multiple calls
  - Constraints to prevent dangerous combinations of tools
- Handles external environment complexity:
  - Rate limits and quotas for APIs and tools
  - Authentication and authorization for tools per user, per agent, or per tenant
  - Throttling and prioritization of tool usage under load","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"State, Memory & Knowledge Management for Agents","scope_included":"- Designs explicit state models for agents:
  - Conversation/session state
  - Task/plan state (steps completed, pending, failed)
  - User profile, preferences, and permissions
  - Global vs per‑session vs per‑tenant state
- Engineers memory mechanisms appropriate to use case:
  - Short‑term/ephemeral memory vs long‑term/persistent memory
  - Episodic memory (past interactions) vs semantic memory (knowledge abstraction)
  - Criteria for when to store, update, or prune memories
- Implements retrieval‑augmented workflows for memory and knowledge:
  - Indexing content (documents, tickets, logs, code, knowledge base articles)
  - Vector search and hybrid search (dense + sparse)
  - Re‑ranking, filtering, and aggregation of retrieved items
  - Using retrieved context responsibly in prompts (context selection, deduplication, compression)
- Chooses and manages knowledge infrastructure:
  - Vector databases, search engines, or embedding‑enhanced relational stores
  - Data modeling for chunks, metadata, and relationships
  - Ingestion pipelines, versioning of knowledge bases, and re‑indexing strategies
- Deals with knowledge freshness and consistency:
  - Strategies for near‑real‑time updates vs batch syncs
  - Handling stale or conflicting information
  - Surfacing provenance and citations to users for transparency
- Prevents memory pathologies:
  - Unbounded growth and cost blow‑up
  - Privacy leaks via over‑aggressive memory storage
  - Cross‑user contamination in multi‑tenant environments","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Safety, Alignment & Guardrails in Production Agents","scope_included":"- Designs multi‑layer safety architectures rather than relying on a single filter:
  - System prompt policies and constraints
  - Model‑side safety settings and policies
  - Input pre‑filters and sanitizers
  - Output post‑filters (rule‑based, classifier‑based, or LLM‑based)
  - Dedicated "safety agents" or moderation layers where appropriate
- Understands common attack vectors and misuses affecting agents:
  - Prompt injection and instruction hijacking
  - Data exfiltration via tools or retrieved documents
  - Jailbreak attempts and obfuscation
  - Tool abuse (e.g., mass email, destructive updates, data scraping)
- Designs defenses suitable for agents with tool access:
  - Separation of data domains and least‑privilege tool permissions
  - Validation of instructions against business policies
  - Allow/deny lists, rate limits, and approval workflows for high‑impact actions
- Implements content and behavior policies:
  - Harassment, hate, self‑harm, illegal activity content filters
  - Enterprise‑specific rules (regulatory, brand, compliance constraints)
  - Handling of personally identifiable information and sensitive data in prompts and logs
- Develops and uses safety evaluation regimes:
  - Red‑teaming and adversarial testing scenarios
  - Ongoing incident review and pattern analysis
  - Metrics for safety violations and policy breach rates
- Designs escalation and override mechanisms:
  - Human‑in‑the‑loop review for risky actions or ambiguous cases
  - Clear user messaging when content or actions are blocked
  - Audit logs for investigation and regulatory reporting","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Reliability, Robustness & Observability of Agents","scope_included":"- Designs agents to behave predictably under real‑world conditions:
  - Handling incomplete or malformed user input gracefully
  - Dealing with upstream system errors, timeouts, and partial failures
  - Clear and helpful fallback responses when the agent cannot safely proceed
- Implements robust error‑handling strategies:
  - Categorization of errors (user, tool, infrastructure, model, orchestration)
  - Structured error propagation, logging, and retry policies
  - Safe aborts, partial rollbacks, and compensation for failed steps
- Builds comprehensive observability for agent behavior:
  - Centralized logging of prompts, responses, tool calls, and context (with privacy controls)
  - Traces for multi‑step plans and cross‑service flows
  - Metrics dashboards for latency, error rates, success/fail, cost, and tool usage
- Uses and extends specialized LLM/agent observability tools and practices:
  - Prompt and run tracing across components
  - Evaluation‑time and runtime analytics for quality and safety
  - Sampling strategies for manual review and labeling
- Designs health checks and SLOs for agents:
  - Uptime and availability per channel or region
  - Latency budgets for different operations or user journeys
  - Quality SLOs (minimum success rate, max safety violation rate)
- Plans for resilience and disaster recovery:
  - Model provider fallbacks and multi‑provider strategies
  - Region and AZ redundancy for critical components
  - Graceful degradation modes (reduced functionality with clear messaging instead of hard failures)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Evaluation, Testing & Quality Assurance","scope_included":"- Treats evaluation as a first‑class, continuous activity rather than a one‑time exercise:
  - Designs evaluation strategies aligned with product and business goals
  - Distinguishes between offline evaluation, pre‑deployment testing, canary/AB, and post‑deployment monitoring
- Uses multiple evaluation methods appropriately:
  - Rule‑based checks and deterministic unit tests where possible
  - Golden sets and labeled test suites for representative tasks
  - LLM‑as‑judge or rubric‑based evaluation for subjective tasks
  - Human evaluation workflows for nuanced or high‑risk output
- Builds agent‑specific testing practices:
  - Unit tests for tools and deterministic components
  - Scenario‑based tests for multi‑step plans and workflows
  - Regression test suites for prompts and configurations
  - Stress and chaos tests for high load and failure conditions
- Designs and tracks quality metrics:
  - Task success and correctness rates per scenario
  - Escalation rates to humans or fallback systems
  - First‑contact resolution vs back‑and‑forth interactions
  - User satisfaction scores and qualitative feedback analysis
- Iterates via evaluation data:
  - Tuning prompts and workflows based on observed failure patterns
  - Updating knowledge sources and tools that frequently cause issues
  - Using experiment platforms to compare models, prompts, and policies
- Ensures test data quality and representativeness:
  - Coverage of edge cases, long‑tail scenarios, and adversarial inputs
  - Representative user personas, languages, and domains as required
  - Periodic refresh of test sets to avoid overfitting","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Performance, Scaling & Cost Optimization","scope_included":"- Designs agents to meet performance requirements at scale:
  - Latency budgets per user journey and internal step
  - Concurrency and throughput targets based on traffic projections
  - Capacity planning for model endpoints, orchestrators, and tool services
- Understands and manages the main performance knobs:
  - Model selection (size, architecture, provider, deployment tier)
  - Context length and prompt size control (prompt optimization, context compression, summarization)
  - Parallel vs sequential tool invocations and sub‑plans
  - Caching strategies for prompts, retrieval results, and intermediate computations
- Implements systematic cost management:
  - Per‑request and per‑task cost estimation
  - Cost visibility and allocation (by feature, tenant, or business unit)
  - Guardrails for max tokens, retries, and tool calls per interaction
  - Budget‑aware behaviors (e.g., use cheaper models for certain steps, escalate only when necessary)
- Designs for horizontal and vertical scaling:
  - Horizontal scaling of stateless components
  - Scalable storage and search for knowledge and memory systems
  - Queues and worker pools to smooth out traffic spikes
- Implements performance testing and tuning cycles:
  - Load tests for peak and burst scenarios
  - Profiling of prompt construction, retrieval, and tool calls
  - Latency breakdown analysis to focus optimizations
- Plans and manages model provisioning:
  - Multi‑model strategies (fast/cheap vs slow/accurate models)
  - Routing rules for different segments, tasks, or tiers
  - Cold‑start mitigation and warm‑pool management for on‑prem or self‑hosted models","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Data, Feedback Loops & Continuous Improvement","scope_included":"- Designs data flows around agent interactions:
  - Structured logging of inputs, outputs, intermediate steps, and outcomes
  - Storage of labeled outcomes (success, failure type, safety incident)
  - Separation and protection of sensitive or private data
- Builds feedback mechanisms into products using agents:
  - User feedback prompts (thumbs up/down, free‑form comments)
  - Implicit feedback signals (task completion, abandonment, escalation)
  - Feedback from human operators who review or correct agent outputs
- Uses data for systematic improvement:
  - Analysis of failure clusters and error typologies
  - Iterative prompt and workflow updates based on actual issues
  - Improvements to retrieval, knowledge sources, and tool coverage
- Connects agent data with ML lifecycle where appropriate:
  - Curation of datasets for fine‑tuning or preference optimization
  - Evaluation of whether fine‑tuning vs better prompting vs better tools is the right lever
  - Guardrails to ensure training data does not violate privacy or policy
- Designs safe experimentation workflows:
  - A/B and multivariate tests for models, prompts, tools, and flows
  - Canary rollouts and staged feature flags
  - Robust experiment analysis and guardrails to prevent regressions in safety or reliability
- Ensures continuous learning is controlled and auditable:
  - Avoids unsupervised online learning that could drift into unsafe behavior
  - Maintains reproducibility of changes (prompts, models, configs, data) over time","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Security, Privacy & Compliance for Agents","scope_included":"- Applies standard application security practices with LLM/agent‑specific nuances:
  - Input validation and sanitization for user and tool inputs
  - Secure coding practices for all deterministic components
  - Protection against injection, XSS, CSRF, SSRF, and other web/API vulnerabilities
- Designs secure use of LLMs and tools:
  - Secrets management for API keys, credentials, and tokens
  - Authentication and authorization models for agent actions
  - Per‑user and per‑role access control on tools and data sources
- Implements privacy‑preserving designs:
  - Data minimization in prompts, logs, and knowledge bases
  - Anonymization or pseudonymization where appropriate
  - Controls for cross‑tenant data separation and isolation
- Handles regulatory and compliance requirements:
  - Requirements for data residency and data retention
  - Handling of PII, PHI, financial and other regulated data types
  - Logging and audit trails suitable for compliance audits
- Manages third‑party model and service risk:
  - Contractual and technical controls for model providers
  - Policies for what data can be sent to external services
  - Vendor risk assessments and contingency plans
- Designs incident response for security and privacy events:
  - Detection of anomalies and potential breaches
  - Triage, containment, notification, and remediation processes
  - Post‑incident reviews and system hardening","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"MLOps & Release Management for Agent Systems","scope_included":"- Integrates agents into modern CI/CD and MLOps practices:
  - Versioning for prompts, models, tools, and workflows
  - Environment promotion (dev, staging, prod) with controlled changes
  - Automated tests and checks as part of pipelines
- Handles model lifecycle as part of agent lifecycle:
  - Model selection, evaluation, approval, and retirement
  - Rolling out new models and rolling back when necessary
  - Managing multiple model versions in parallel during migrations or experiments
- Manages configuration and feature flags for agents:
  - Runtime configuration of prompts, safety policies, tool access
  - Dark launches and controlled feature rollouts
  - Tenant‑specific or region‑specific configurations
- Ensures reproducibility and traceability:
  - Ability to reconstruct which model, prompt, config, and tools produced a given response
  - Audit trails for changes to critical components
  - Change management processes with risk assessment
- Coordinates with traditional software delivery:
  - Aligning agent changes with upstream/downstream service releases
  - Ensuring backward compatibility for APIs and data contracts
  - Handling schema evolution for tools and knowledge systems","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Tooling & Platform Ecosystem (Frameworks, Infrastructure, Ops)","scope_included":"- Proficient with major agentic and LLM orchestration frameworks and libraries:
  - Chains, workflows, and agents abstractions
  - Tool definitions, routing, and dynamic selection
  - Framework‑built evaluation, monitoring, and observability features
- Capable of working within or extending platform‑level agent offerings:
  - Hosted agent runtimes and assistants platforms
  - Enterprise AI platforms that provide RAG, tool orchestration, and policy layers
- Uses or integrates with vector search, retrieval, and knowledge platforms:
  - Vector databases, embedding services, and search engines
  - Pipelines for ingestion, chunking, and indexing
- Operates within standard cloud and infra environments for production systems:
  - Kubernetes or container platforms to deploy agent services
  - Queues, event buses, and schedulers for asynchronous work
  - API gateways, service meshes, and load balancers
- Employs observability, experimentation, and analytics tooling:
  - General‑purpose metrics, logs, and traces platforms
  - LLM‑specific tracing and evaluation platforms
  - Product analytics for user behavior and agent impact
- Selects tools and platforms based on clear criteria:
  - Vendor lock‑in, portability, and extensibility
  - Integration with existing enterprise stack and compliance requirements
  - Operational complexity vs benefits","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Stakeholder Management & Product Thinking","scope_included":"- Works closely with product managers, designers, and domain experts:
  - Translates business goals and user needs into agent capabilities and constraints
  - Co‑designs user journeys and UX patterns around agents (chat, copilots, background agents)
  - Identifies appropriate success metrics tied to business outcomes
- Collaborates effectively with engineers and data teams:
  - Aligns on contracts and SLAs for tools and external systems
  - Ensures data pipelines and knowledge sources meet quality and freshness needs
  - Communicates technical trade‑offs and risks clearly
- Engages with legal, risk, and compliance stakeholders:
  - Aligns safety and policy controls with organizational standards
  - Participates in risk assessments and governance processes
- Practices responsible product thinking for agents:
  - Identifies misuse and unintended consequences
  - Designs transparent experiences (explaining capabilities, limits, and data usage)
  - Ensures agents augment, not undermine, human workflows and roles
- Provides realistic expectations and education to non‑technical stakeholders:
  - Clarifies what agents can and cannot reliably do
  - Explains uncertainty, probabilities, and fallibility of LLMs
  - Guides prioritization based on feasibility and risk","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Documentation, Knowledge Sharing & Mentoring","scope_included":"- Produces clear, comprehensive documentation for agent systems:
  - Architectural diagrams and component descriptions
  - Prompt design rationales, safety policies, and configuration guides
  - Runbooks for operations, incident response, and on‑call procedures
- Documents behavior and limitations of agents for internal and external audiences:
  - Known failure modes and edge cases
  - Expected user interaction patterns and best practices
  - Escalation paths and human‑in‑the‑loop processes
- Contributes to shared knowledgebases and playbooks:
  - Design patterns and anti‑patterns for agents
  - Internal libraries and reusable components for common tasks
  - Post‑mortems and lessons learned from incidents or launches
- Mentors and supports less experienced engineers:
  - Reviews designs, prompts, and code with constructive feedback
  - Teaches fundamentals of LLMs, RAG, and agent architectures
  - Helps others understand safety, evaluation, and reliability practices
- Advocates for and models good experimentation and measurement practices:
  - Encourages data‑driven iteration over intuition‑only changes
  - Shares evaluation methodologies and tools with the team","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Process, Governance & Risk Management","scope_included":"- Participates in or helps shape governance processes for agents:
  - Checkpoints for safety, privacy, and compliance reviews
  - Approval processes for new tools, data sources, and high‑impact actions
  - Playbooks for major changes to models, prompts, or policies
- Incorporates risk analysis into design and implementation:
  - Identifies key failure modes and their potential impacts
  - Applies risk mitigation strategies proportionate to risk level
  - Balances innovation with robustness and safety constraints
- Works within organizational processes for quality and release management:
  - Design reviews and architecture boards
  - Change advisory and incident review boards where applicable
- Helps establish standards and best practices for agent systems:
  - Coding standards and patterns for agent components
  - Common logging, observability, and evaluation baselines
  - Guidelines for when and how to use agents vs traditional automation
- Ensures accountability and traceability:
  - Clear ownership of agents and their behaviors
  - Processes for user support, incident reporting, and remediation follow‑through","is_non_core_section":true}]}
